ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for apple juice (FOODON_03301710) but no specific entry for apple-and-aloe-vera juice drink with pulp. Closest defensible parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fruit nectars and fruit juice drinks
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 category A00LC covers fruit nectars and juice drinks; no specific apple-aloe vera subcategory exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for apple and aloe vera juice drink. Ontology gap noted.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of apple and aloe vera juice drink is approximately 250 ml (about one standard bottle or cup), providing around 103 kcal and 25.5 g of sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Apple and Aloe Vera Juice Drink — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Apple and Aloe Vera Juice Drink (cr_id D03070001). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for apple-and-aloe-vera juice drink. GI estimated at 55 based on apple juice GI values (range 40–60, median ~55) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The University of Sydney GI Database classifies GI 55 as low (threshold: low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Aloe vera pulp contributes negligible carbohydrate and is not expected to materially alter GI. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (55 × 10.14 × 2.5) / 100 = 13.9, rounded to 14.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (10.2 g/100 g) in a liquid beverage exceeds the 5 g/100 g sugar threshold for beverages; chronic high sugar intake independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened juice drink; despite its low sodium (21 mg/100 g), the 10.2 g/100 g sugar load independently elevates cardiovascular and blood pressure risk and should be replaced with plain water or unsweetened beverages.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened liquid beverage with 10.2 g sugar/100 g and only 0.08 g dietary fibre/100 g; liquid sugars are absorbed rapidly, producing significant postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely; the 10.2 g/100 g sugar in liquid form with negligible fibre (0.08 g/100 g) causes rapid glycaemic spikes, and whole fresh apple with intact fibre is a far preferable alternative if fruit intake is desired.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar content (10.2 g/100 g) in a processed juice drink contributes to excess caloric intake, hypertriglyceridaemia, and metabolic risk factors associated with cardiovascular disease.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this sugar-sweetened juice drink, as regular intake of free sugars at this concentration is associated with elevated triglycerides, weight gain, and increased CVD risk per WHO 2015 free-sugar guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High liquid sugar content (10.2 g/100 g) with negligible fibre promotes systemic inflammation; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, or whole-food fibre >3 g) are present to offset the pro-inflammatory sugar load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage; the high free-sugar content in liquid form promotes inflammatory mediators, and the trace aloe vera and vitamin C content is insufficient to confer anti-inflammatory benefit at this concentration.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Apple and Aloe Vera Juice Drink is a commercially processed, sugar-sweetened beverage consisting primarily of apple juice blended with aloe vera pulp. It is a clear-to-slightly-opaque liquid with a sweet, mildly tart apple flavour and occasional soft aloe vera gel pieces that provide a subtle chewy texture. The drink is widely available in Singapore as a packaged ready-to-drink beverage and is popular as a refreshing soft drink option. Nutritionally, it provides 41 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates (10.14 g/100 g) as the dominant macronutrient, almost entirely from sugars (10.2 g/100 g). Protein (0.04 g/100 g) and fat (0.0 g/100 g) are negligible. Dietary fibre is minimal at 0.08 g/100 g. Sodium is low at 21 mg/100 g, and calcium is notably present at 60 mg/100 g, likely from fortification. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened drink and is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high free-sugar content and rapid glycaemic impact. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 55 (low by University of Sydney classification), based on apple juice analogues. A standard 250 ml serving delivers approximately 25.5 g of sugar and a glycemic load of approximately 14, making it a significant source of rapidly absorbed free sugars. It is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id D03070001) with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous source (apple juice, University of Sydney GI Database) with documented basis. Local-language names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to support multilingual retrieval in the Singapore market context.
notes: fat_g is naturally zero by product formulation, confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D03070001. iron_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this product. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate based on apple juice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for apple-and-aloe-vera juice drink was found in approved sources. gi_category is 'low' per University of Sydney GI classification threshold (low ≤55). Calcium at 60 mg/100 g is notably elevated for a juice drink and may reflect fortification; this value is preserved as reported by HPB. Although this is a generic commercial beverage rather than a traditional Singaporean dish, local-language names in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi have been provided to support multilingual retrieval for the Singapore market. All local-language alias strings appear verbatim in the flat aliases array (q8 consistency check passed).
